system_prompt:
You are extracting the structural outline of a guitar method book from its front matter.

The user will give you the verbatim text of the first ~20 pages of a book.
Identify the chapters or major sections of the book (whichever the book uses
— some method books use "Lesson", "Chapter", "Part", "Section", "Unit").

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "chapters": [
      { "n": 1, "title": "...", "start_page": <int>, "end_page": <int> },
      ...
  ] }

Rules:
- `n` is the 1-indexed chapter number you assign (independent of the book's
  own numbering — book may start at 0 or use Roman numerals).
- `title` is the chapter's title as it appears in the book.
- `start_page` is the first page of the chapter; `end_page` is the last.
- If the front matter doesn't list page numbers, infer from the chapter
  headings you see — start_page is where the chapter HEADING appears, and
  end_page is one less than the next chapter's start_page (or the book's
  last page for the final chapter).
- Skip the table of contents itself, the title page, the copyright page,
  the author bio — they are not "chapters" in this sense.
- If the book is short and not subdivided, output a single chapter covering
  the entire content.
user_prompt:
Total pages in the book: 30.
First 20 pages follow. Identify chapters.

<<<TEXT
       An approach to Jazz Improvisation

          A method by Peter Bernstein




         Master’s Thesis by Nico Moreno

              New York University

              Jazz studies Program

The Roots of Jazz: An Original American Art Form

         Professor Dr. David Schroeder

              New York City, 2023


This paper aims to document and organize Peter Bernstein's approach to jazz improvisation.​    3
Recurrent themes during lessons​                                                               3
Concepts​                                                                                      4
             Jazz as a language and the importance of narrative​                               4
             How to learn a tune​                                                              5
             The primacy of the melody​                                                        6
             The relation between chords and melody​                                           7
             Language, rhythm and meaning​                                                     8
             Comping and supporting the melody​                                                9
             Dressing the notes​                                                               9
             Spend time comping​                                                              11
             Rhythm of the comping​                                                           11
             Harmonic movement tension and substitutions​                                     11
             Expanding the vocabulary​                                                        13
                  An open approach​                                                           14
                  Creating ideas around target notes​                                         15
                  Creating ideas from a shape​                                                21
             Melodic target notes​                                                            24
             The narrative of the solo​                                                       25
             Cadenza​                                                                         26
             Technique and flexibility: Dynamics, Phrasing and Articulation​                  27
Final thoughts​                                                                               29
             Unique and narrow aesthetic path developed over the years. Opposite to unrelated
             explorations​                                                                  29
             A pure exponent of the jazz tradition​                                           30




​


                                                                                               2


This paper aims to document and organize Peter Bernstein's approach to jazz
improvisation.

I have been studying with Peter Bernstein for about a year at New York University, and all the
information presented in this document is derived from the knowledge and insights he shared
during our sessions.

To ensure an accurate record of our discussions, I have obtained Bernstein's consent to record the
audio from our lessons. This has proven to be an invaluable tool in comprehending Bernstein's
unique approach to music. The availability of these recordings has allowed me to carefully
consider and explore the intricacies of his ideas. While my focus during the lessons is often
concentrated on specific areas, revisiting the recordings allows me to connect Bernstein's
teachings with different concepts and perspectives.

Bernstein is widely recognized as one of the most accomplished practitioners of jazz guitar and
jazz as a whole. My recordings serve as the primary resource for this research project, as I
believe Bernstein embodies a living repository of jazz tradition while also pushing the
boundaries of jazz guitar as a revolutionary exponent. This paper endeavors to capture some of
his profound ideas and insights.

Recurrent themes during lessons

Throughout my lessons, I observed recurring themes that Bernstein addressed, which may be
explored from various perspectives during different sessions.

Bernstein himself acknowledges that his teaching approach lacks a strict methodical structure.
Instead, he emphasizes the importance of contextualizing ideas within specific musical pieces.
Rarely does he explain a concept without relating it to a musical context. This is why he
recommended organizing his teachings into a document. He has a distinct approach to working
with musical ideas, and his manner of discussing them is informal. This informal approach
reflects his own learning journey, which involved attending music school for a period but
primarily gaining knowledge through playing and touring with revered figures in the jazz
tradition, rather than in an academic setting. Therefore, the purpose of this paper is twofold: to
organize the recurring themes discussed by Bernstein and to present musical examples through
diagrams and notation. This approach will provide the reader with a more structured
understanding of Bernstein's perspectives on improvisation.




                                                                                                     3


Concepts

One remarkable aspect of Bernstein's creativity is his ability to perceive a concept from various
perspectives. He employs different analogies to convey the same idea, allowing us, his students,
to approach those concepts with distinct flavors and subtle variations. Ultimately, this leads to a
more diverse and captivating storytelling experience during improvisations. It is important to
note that all of these ideas were consistently discussed within the context of specific tunes. As
we delved into a particular song during a lesson, Bernstein would explain a concept from a
perspective that aligned with the nuances and relevance of that specific tune. In subsequent
lessons when exploring different songs, he would revisit the same concept, but from a slightly
different angle, tailored to the intricacies of the new piece. With this in mind, my aim in
capturing these ideas is to gather and synthesize the concepts discussed over multiple lessons,
presenting them in a comprehensive manner. By doing so, I hope to provide a valuable resource
for understanding and applying Bernstein's insightful teachings.


Jazz as a language and the importance of narrative

According to Bernstein, the way some schools teach jazz is problematic in his view. These
schools often focus on providing students with chord progressions and corresponding scales,
without emphasizing the concept of narrative, meaning, and effectively communicating a story
through musical ideas. While the schools offer materials, they often neglect to explain the
significance and underlying meaning behind those ideas. For Bernstein, jazz improvisation is
akin to having a conversation, where one must possess a vocabulary and an understanding of
how to combine those musical "words" to convey specific ideas. It involves using musical
sentences and paragraphs to organize and express meaning, ultimately telling a story.

Bernstein finds it somewhat unnatural how some schools approach music education. He draws a
comparison to how children learn a language, emphasizing that they don't learn to speak from a
book but rather by listening to others and imitating their speech, gradually acquiring words and
learning how to convey ideas using those words. In jazz education, he observes that the focus on
learning scales that fit over chords often neglects the concept of ideas, which he likens to words.
Without clear ideas or words, it becomes challenging to convey meaningful musical expressions.
It's akin to trying to generate words as you speak, rather than learning and creatively combining
words by understanding the syntax of the language. The ultimate goal is to delve deeper into
expression, meaning, and the art of storytelling.

Bernstein stresses the importance of practicality in using musical language, not merely reciting
all the known words without expressing anything meaningful. Particularly in the early stages of
learning, one should develop the skill to use the language in a practical manner, knowing which
phrases to employ in different musical situations. Key to this process is understanding what
                                                                                                      4


constitutes a musical phrase or idea and recognizing what distinguishes a song from a collection
of notes that happen to fit the chords. He conveyed to me that each tune is like a poem, teaching
us the syntax of language and how to assemble words and set up rhymes, and every song has
something to teach about this music. He drew an analogy to songs being akin to plays or movies,
where the melody represents the story itself—the core element. The customs, lights, and props
serve as complementary elements that need to be properly oriented towards the story.

To further illustrate this concept of narrative, he offered an analogy, comparing learning to tell
stories from a tune to a comedian crafting jokes. Comedians learn numerous common jokes,
allowing them to understand setups, narratives, and punchlines that resonate with the audience.
Similarly, songs teach us how to tell relatable stories. I recall asking him once if he transcribed
solos, and he mentioned that he did it when he was in music school upon request, but he stopped
doing it afterward. He prefers to learn small phrases. However, more importantly, he emphasized
that learning tunes is more significant than learning solos or phrases because the ideas found in
jazz compositions are the best ideas the composers could come up with, which is why they
decided to create a tune around them. Therefore, studying tunes and dedicating time to exploring
those phrases is a great way to comprehend the most beautiful aspects of the language.


How to learn a tune

I have always been impressed by the vast repertoire of tunes he knows. Even before I started
taking lessons with him, I would come across versions played by him for almost every tune I
wanted to learn. Now, during our lessons, I have realized that his knowledge extends even
further, including tunes I haven't even heard of. Bernstein has immersed himself in so many
tunes that learning new ones has become second nature to him. His ability to analyze and
internalize them is truly remarkable. He is widely recognized for his exceptional rendition of
standard tunes, and I’m constantly amazed by the profound level of analysis he applies to each
one we explore in our lessons. Bernstein possesses a masterful understanding of the fundamental
elements that contribute to the greatness of a tune, and he skillfully expands upon these elements
to construct captivating narratives. These can range from simple rhythmic figures to more
intricate aspects like the core notes of a melody.

He likes to perform solo versions of the tunes we play during our lessons. In these instances, he
takes advantage of the freedom that comes with playing alone, allowing his exploration to take
him to a multitude of musical destinations. It’s in these moments that I truly grasp the depth of
his artistic explorations. I’ve come to realize that what we hear on stage or in recordings is just a
piece of the vast range of ideas Bernstein can delve into when interpreting a tune. During some
of the lessons, Bernstein enjoyed teaching me tunes in a primarily oral traditional manner. He
would play the sections one by one and elucidate his thought process when learning the tune. We
didn't rely on lead sheets or any type of charts during these sessions. I found these lessons

                                                                                                    5


exceptionally enlightening since Bernstein demonstrated his approach to learning a tune while
simultaneously teaching me a new piece of music.

During some of our lessons, I would bring in my original compositions, which were heavily
influenced by jazz standards. Most of them followed the typical 32-bar structures with AABA or
ABAC forms, using common jazz chord progressions. Occasionally, I would venture into more
unconventional harmonic territories, but the overall style remained traditional. I was curious to
see how Bernstein would approach learning and interpreting new music, and the way he
approached it was very insightful.

Initially, he would take a somewhat conservative approach to the chord voicings, mostly sticking
to basic shell voicings, with occasional signature chord runs where he knew they would work.
However, as we progressed through the tune, after playing the third or fourth chorus, he would
begin to explore further. He would introduce more extended chords, harmonic substitutions, and
experiment with rhythm and textures. By the end of the tune, it would sound as if he had been
practicing it for years.

I noticed that by that point he had a complete understanding of how the tune was constructed
harmonically and melodically. He could identify the structural notes of the melody and
understand how the melody and harmony interacted. I noticed this because Bernstein would
provide me with suggestions on how to make it more interesting and align it with the thousands
of great tunes he knows. He would often demonstrate different approaches by playing sections of
the tune himself, combining chords and melody in a solo guitar arrangement. He would then
showcase alternative ways to harmonize a melody note or present different compositional
pathways he would have explored.


The primacy of the melody

Studying with Bernstein has revealed to me his mastery of the fundamentals. He possesses a
keen sense of what is important and essential in a tune, with the melody standing out as a crucial
element.

I'm constantly impressed by the level of detail he employs when playing the melody. His choice
of phrasing and articulation is always deeply informed by the original versions of the tune,
particularly in hard bop compositions where the melody's phrasing is often fixed due to multiple
instruments playing in unison or harmonizing the main melody. Moreover, his ability to translate
the nuances of horn interpretations to the guitar, as we explored in the technique chapter, is well
thought out.



                                                                                                  6


During one lesson, I shared a solo guitar arrangement of Charles Mingus' blues tune, "Nostalgia
in Times Square." However, I wasn't satisfied with how it sounded as I attempted to play the
melody and chords simultaneously. Bernstein interrupted me and emphasized that the most
important thing, regardless of the setting, is to never sacrifice the phrasing of the melody. He
proceeded to demonstrate a beautiful example, articulating the melody and playing the chords
during the spaces suggested by the melody. His approach effectively conveyed the essence of the
tune.

He explained that some guitarists try to play everything simultaneously, but in doing so, they
sacrifice the feel and phrasing, resulting in stiffened music. Instead, he appreciates players who
infuse feeling into the notes of the melody, rather than, as he put it, "trying to juggle while riding
an unicycle."

On another occasion, Bernstein asked me to perform a solo piece, and I chose "The Days of
Wine and Roses." While playing, I inadvertently forgot a part of the melody and substituted it
with chord movements to fill the gap. He stopped me and pointed out that I was trying to rush to
step 12 without adequately accomplishing step 1, which is mastering the melody. Drawing again
a parallel to the analogy of a play or a movie, for him trying to play a chord run without honoring
the melody is like a car chase without proper context within the story.

For Bernstein, the initial step in learning a song is to be able to play the melody as a monophonic
instrument and accurately deliver the story. He also emphasized the importance of the
relationship between the melody and lyrics, noting how understanding the words helps to grasp
the connection between syllables and notes.


The relation between chords and melody

As I mentioned earlier in this chapter, Bernstein would teach me new tunes using an oral
approach rather than relying on lead sheets. One of the main points he emphasized during these
sessions was the significance of understanding the relationship between the melody and the
chords.

To teach me a song, he would start by playing a solo take where he clearly played the melody
and rhythms. This allowed me to hear the tune before he began teaching me section by section. If
the melody of the tune was straightforward, he would teach me the chords and melody
simultaneously, always highlighting the importance of comprehending their connection.

For instance, during one lesson, he taught me "The End of a Love Affair." The first chord in the
tune is a Gm7, and the melody features an A note, which is the 9th of the Gm7 chord. Instead of
directly telling me what that was, he played that little section and asked me what I heard. I

                                                                                                     7


played a Gm9 chord with the 9th on top, and he described it as a "flavor" or a "spice." According
to him, one should know exactly what that spice will add to the mix. He also explained that an
alternative way to approach that section is by playing a D7sus with an A on top, which would be
a variation of the original flavor—still "spicy," but "a little bit more bitter." Bernstein thinks
about chords and tensions in terms of relating them to the senses and something more
memorable, rather than as a collection of abstract names and numbers. This way, he can hear
certain chords and associate them with similar flavors, just like how one can find the same spice
but with different nuances of freshness or sweetness.

On another occasion, he taught me the song "Simone" by Frank Foster, which features a more
active melody and a faster tempo. For this song, he first taught me the chord progression, and
then he had me play the chords while he played the melody. The goal was for me to hear the
complete song and grasp the rhythms of the melody. Afterward, he taught me the melody. At this
stage, he suggested that I think about the notes that fit the chord while figuring out the melody by
ear.

Moreover, when the melody targeted a specific note of the chord, he encouraged me to take note
of it as something to remember and as a guideline for deciding which types of chords to use. For
example, in the harmony of this particular tune, which resembles a minor blues in D, an A7
chord always resolves to D minor. In such contexts, where a minor I (one) chord serves as the
tonic of the song, Bernstein explained that it's usually more appropriate to play a Dm6 chord,
where the major 6th replaces the minor 7th within the chord. However, in this particular song,
the melody plays the minor 7th of the chord, so he suggested playing more of a Dm7-type chord,
possibly using quartal voicings to emphasize the 7th and the 11th.

For Bernstein, it is essential to learn the melody and chords together or, at the very least, to
combine them as soon as possible to truly grasp the meaning and construction of the song.


Language, rhythm and meaning

Whenever Bernstein taught me a song or a phrase, he always encouraged me to pay close
attention to the rhythm after learning the notes. He believed that rhythm gives meaning to the
musical idea. While the notes are certainly a part of it, it is the way they fit within the measure
and the spaces between them that imbues those notes with significance as an idea. He drew an
analogy to language and punctuation, explaining that saying "Hello, nice to see you" is different
from saying "Hello nice to, see you." The former makes sense, while the latter is awkward and
unclear in meaning.




                                                                                                      8


Comping and supporting the melody

Bernstein is renowned for his comping style, and one of the highlights of my lessons with him is
playing alongside him. His comping is incredibly supportive and inspiring, and it makes me
sound much better than my actual level of playing.

During one lesson, while teaching me Horace Silver's "Strollin'," I attempted to play extended
chords while he played the melody. However, he stopped me and advised me to start with basic
chords first—just the root, third, and seventh of each chord. He encouraged me to focus on
listening to the melody and understanding the relationship between the basic chords and the
melody before making more informed decisions about incorporating chord extensions.

This example showcases his approach to comping and playing in general. Bernstein ensures that
he can create something tasteful using the fundamental elements before exploring new ideas. It's
akin to working with layers, where he prioritizes building a solid foundation with the basic layers
before progressing to new techniques or embellishments.


Dressing the notes

During a session while playing "How Deep Is the Ocean," I inquired about Bernstein's approach
to practicing comping. He shared with me the concept of ensuring that the chords played always
enhance the melody, likening it to "dressing the notes." According to Bernstein, you can make
each note feel good by giving it the right "outfit" and making it feel important.

To illustrate this idea, he focused on the ending of "How Deep Is the Ocean," where the melody
resolves to an F while the chord is an Fm7. Bernstein explained that if the bass were to play the
same melody note, it could make the melody feel weak. Instead, he prefers to play a B7#11
chord, where the F becomes the #11, allowing the melody note to have a different "outfit" that
enhances its impact and makes it feel good.


Example:




                 Example 1:”dressing the f note” on “How Deep Is The Ocean”



                                                                                                    9


Then, he proceeded to explain how one can create their own guide tones for each chord, aiming
to establish a strong and captivating melodic line. Afterwards, you have the freedom to
determine how to fill in the remaining notes of the chord.

First he played these guide tones, with the bass:




          Example 2: bass and guide notes for first chords of “How deep is the ocean”


Furthermore, he elaborated that once you have established a solid structure with strong guide
notes and bass notes, you have the flexibility to incorporate any notes in between. The idea is to
consider notes that would "dress better" the guide tones, enhancing their impact and making
them feel pleasing.

So he played this by applying that principle:




   Example n 3: “Dressing the notes” for own guide tones on first chords of “How deep is the
                                           ocean”




Another aspect that Bernstein enjoys doing is reharmonizing sections of the melody when he
anticipates the notes I'm going to play in the song's heads. This creates intriguing harmonic
movements, "dressing the notes," and enhancing their sound while still respecting the essential
harmonic narrative of the song.


                                                                                                  10


Moreover, he had a remarkable understanding of whatever I played during my solos. When my
harmonic ideas were clear, he would follow along, making the notes I played sound even better.
He expressed that it was highly engaging for him to play with musicians who could grasp and
respond to his ideas, and he believed that the greatest musicians were those who could pick up on
the ideas of their fellow bandmates and create a profound musical conversation.


Spend time comping

During another lesson, Bernstein shared with me his practice of recording himself while comping
for extended periods of time. He would then listen back to the recordings and play over them.
According to him, this practice was highly valuable for two reasons. Firstly, it allowed him to
dedicate a significant amount of time to practicing comping. Secondly, it provided an
opportunity to experience how it feels to play over his own comping, enabling him to assess the
interest and effectiveness of his comping ideas based on his own playing experience.

He also recounted his experiences of playing in sessions when he was younger, where they
would perform a single tune, chorus after chorus, for extended durations. In these situations, he
had to constantly come up with new ideas on the bandstand to keep the music moving forward.
This challenge proved to be beneficial in developing his improvisational ideas and expanding his
comping repertoire.


Rhythm of the comping

Bernstein emphasized the importance of rhythm in his comping, considering it to be equally vital
as the chords he played. He believed that finding captivating rhythms that propelled the music
forward was a key aspect. In one lesson, he showcased this idea by incorporating the iconic
intro's rhythm from "Star Eyes" by Charlie Parker into his comping over the soloist. This
rhythmic figure not only added a pleasing feel to the music but also served as a motif for
variation and development.

While playing alongside Bernstein, I noticed his exceptional rhythmic support in his comping
and his keen understanding of my playing. Even when I made occasional mistakes or
unintentionally affected the timing, he would gracefully adjust and slow down or speed up with
me, ensuring that the music continued to feel good. His deep sense of groove allowed him to
flexibly adapt and mold the groove to suit the nuances of my playing, showcasing both his deep
understanding of groove and his ability to respond and connect as a musician.




                                                                                               11


Harmonic movement tension and substitutions

According to Bernstein, jazz music revolves around the interplay of tension and resolution, or
V's and I's with various flavors. The crucial skill to develop is the ability to transition creatively
between these chord progressions.

He explained to me that harmony, in his view, involves understanding how to navigate from one
chord to another. Chords are not static entities, as they may sometimes appear on a lead sheet,
but rather tools for manipulating tension and resolution. To move from one sound to another, he
employs a wide range of passing chords and interweaves little melodies in between.

This approach is what makes Bernstein's harmonic ideas distinctive and expressive. His ability to
transition from a I to a V using different passing chords and incorporating melodic fragments is
highly creative and propels the music forward.

Additionally, Bernstein emphasized the importance of playing chords within a single chord.
When a tune calls for a chord to be sustained over 4 or 2 measures, it becomes essential to
explore the play between harmonic tension and resolution within that chord. Also utilizing space
and rhythm effectively is key while doing this.

According to Bernstein, the tension is where the fun and creativity in music truly come alive. It
is in these moments that you can explore various ways to navigate and shape that tension. This
approach is why Bernstein's comping is so enjoyable and engaging to play with.

In one particular lesson, while playing "Ornithology" by Charlie Parker, he demonstrated his
preference for not playing the roots of the I (one) chords. Instead, he would opt for an I69 chord
derived from the V of the respective I (one) chord. This choice adds a unique tension and flavor
to the I (one) chord, not resolving the tension fully.

This is the example he played for me:




      Example n. 1: continuing the tension by playing the V on the bass over the changes of
                                         “Ornithology”


                                                                                                     12


According to Bernstein, avoiding an excessive emphasis on the I (one) chord is a wonderful way
to maintain a sense of forward momentum in the music. He believes that when the I chord is
overemphasized, it can give the impression that the song is being dragged or held back.

Another thing that he likes to do is to find substitutions for some chords of the tune that he
knows will work well when the soloist is playing. For example, he showed me how he
sometimes likes to play the ii V a tritone up or dawn the original during the dominant chords that
resolve. This is like playing the related ii chord of the tritone substitution.
​
All of these aspects worked exceptionally well within the harmonic context of his playing, and it
is crucial to discover excellent voice leading to transition smoothly into and out of those
harmonic progressions. Additionally, rhythm plays a vital role, particularly in the case of the ii V
with a tritone up, as playing it in the incorrect position within the bar can create a sense of
awkwardness. Bernstein, being a master of these substitutions, demonstrates a remarkable ability
to apply them in a manner that enhances the overall musical experience.

During our lessons, Bernstein often spoke about Jim Hall and his perspective on music.
Bernstein likened creating good voice leading to composing for a choir, drawing parallels
between the two. Jim Hall had a unique way of envisioning each string on the guitar as an
individual, aiming to bring joy to all by providing them with beautiful melodies to sing. Jim
would advise Bernstein to consider how a single voice expands into two, then three, and so on,
forming organic textures that fluctuate in size. These concepts hold great significance in
comprehending Bernstein's approach to solo guitar arrangements and comping. His comping
stands out as exceptionally unique and supportive, stemming from the combination of various
elements. His exquisite sound, skillful use of dynamics, and intricate interplay of these elements
make it challenging to replicate his comping style. The complexity lies in how these components
are intricately connected to one another, much like a skilled chef who takes different ingredients,
combines them, and applies various techniques to create a delightful dish. Bernstein's comping,
like a culinary masterpiece, is a result of his artistry and the thoughtful intertwining of these
elements.


Expanding the vocabulary

For Bernstein, creativity lies in taking good musical ideas and creating variations that convey the
same meaning but with different flavors. It involves honing the craft of generating multiple ideas
from a single starting point, developing the ability to produce ten new ideas from one and then
ten more from those ten. Bernstein uses the analogy of greetings to illustrate the point—knowing
one greeting like "yo, what's up" may only work in very informal situations, but depending on
the context and the person you're addressing, you'll want to have a range of different greetings at


                                                                                                 13


your disposal. Therefore, it is vital to have a deeper connection to the language of music,
understanding the context and adapting to it effectively.

During our lessons, he demonstrated various techniques for deriving multiple ideas from a core
musical concept. Here are a few examples:

       An open approach

This method involves taking the overall structure of the melodic line as a starting point and
freely experimenting without many constraints. The primary guide in this process is the ear,
which helps in discovering variations that retain the essence of the original while introducing
distinct flavors. For Bernstein, it's akin to constructing phrases using a recipe as inspiration.

This approach represents the utmost freedom to explore various resolution points while
preserving the core structure. It allows for the option of either maintaining the original shape or
introducing changes while still honoring the resolution points. This method holds the most
abstract nature according to Bernstein, as it lacks limitations when it comes to creating these
variations.

Examples:

Here Bernstein was creating variations over a D altered phrase resolving to G major 7. This is the
original line:




                                           Example n. 1: Original line
   Variations:




                                      Example n. 2: Variation

                                                                                                    14


Notice how the main concept or flavor of the line is to land on the Bb (b6) of the first note of the
D7alt, then Bernstein plays around with the other elements.




                                      Example n. 3: Variation




                                      Example n. 4: Variation




                                      Example n. 5: Variation




          Creating ideas around target notes

This alternative method involves crafting phrases that center around a particular note of the
chord. This selected note can be any fundamental note or extension of the chord, and it serves as
the central element of the phrase.

                                                                                                  15


Examples:

Here Bernstein was emphasizing the note D over a G major 7 chord, which is the 5th of the
chord.




                       Example n. 1: emphasizing the D over a G chord




                       Example n. 2: emphasizing the D over a G chord




                       Example n. 3: emphasizing the D over a G chord




                                                                                            16


                        Example n. 4: emphasizing the D over a G chord


Here Bernstein was emphasizing the note D over a C minor 7 chord, which is the 9th of the
chord.

He chose the note D for both examples to also explain how a note can have a different flavor
depending on the harmonic context.




                       Example n. 1: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




                       Example n. 2: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




                       Example n. 3: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




                                                                                               17


Example n. 4: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




Example n. 5: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




Example n. 7: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




Example n. 7: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord




                                                  18


The next step in this process is to expand upon the previous method by selecting two notes and
creating phrases that revolve around them. This approach adds an extra layer of complexity and
introduces a different quality to the lines.

Here Bernstein was emphasizing the note D and F over a C minor 7 chord, which are the 9th and
the 11th of the chord.




                   Example n. 1: emphasizing the D and an F over a Cm chord




                   Example n. 2: emphasizing the D and an F over a Cm chord


For Bernstein, this exercise holds tremendous significance, and he highly encourages applying
this concept to all degrees of the scale. By doing so, you become intimately familiar with the
unique sound and character of each note, allowing you to deeply internalize the essence of every
tension in your imagination.

Furthermore, he explains that the true depth of this exercise lies in the understanding that when
you focus on a specific degree of the scale, you have the freedom to use the entire chromatic
scale to target that note. Every note can relate to any other note within the context of any chord.
Bernstein employs an analogy to describe this approach as a vibrant party dedicated to the target
note. It's a celebration where not only can you invite your family members (the corresponding
scale notes), but also your friends and neighbors (the outside notes). The combination of these
elements brings forth a diverse and dynamic atmosphere, giving the party a unique vibe.



                                                                                                 19


Here Bernstein was emphasizing the note D over a C minor 7 chord, which is 9th of the chord.




                Example n. 1: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord. Original line.


   Then he adds the chromatic notes (not family members)




           Example n. 2: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord using chromatic notes.


This is another line emphasizing the note D over a C minor 7 chord, which is 9th of the chord.




                Example n. 3: emphasizing the D over a Cm chord. Original line.

Then he adds the chromatic notes (not family members):
